conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first check the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have one child – my son Alex. He's 17 and lives at home with me here in Bristol.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask:

Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, college courses, or an approved training program)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He left school and decided he wanted to work instead.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I need to ask:

Is Alex currently working, and if so, how many hours per week does he work?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's got a job at a local warehouse. He's working full-time there now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Just to confirm - when you say full-time, does that mean Alex is working 24 hours or more per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. He's doing more than 24 hours a week there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I'm afraid you would **not be eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why: While Child Benefit can normally continue for young people aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training, the eligibility stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more per week and is no longer in approved education or training.

Since Alex is 17, has left school, is not in any approved education or training, and is working more than 24 hours per week at the warehouse, you would not qualify for Child Benefit for him under current rules.

If Alex's circumstances were to change in the future - for example, if he were to reduce his working hours and enter approved education or training - it would be worth reconsidering eligibility at that time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has left education without being in approved training. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you can normally continue to receive Child Benefit for a child aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training. However, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex is working more than 24 hours per week at the warehouse and is not in any approved education or training, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for him.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives at home in Bristol. While children aged 16-19 can qualify for Child Benefit if they remain in approved education or training, Alex has left school and is not in any education or training. He is working full-time (more than 24 hours per week) at a warehouse. Under UK Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops when a child aged 16 or 17 starts paid work for 24 hours or more per week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex is working more than 24 hours per week at the warehouse and is not in any approved education or training, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for him.

While children aged 16-19 can qualify for Child Benefit if they remain in approved education or training, Alex has left school and is not in any education or training. He is working full-time (more than 24 hours per week) at a warehouse. Under UK Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops when a child aged 16 or 17 starts paid work for 24 hours or more per week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
